topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into the quiet hum of my evening. A profile picture materialized: a distorted, sneering grin I hadn't seen in fifteen years. My stomach clenched, a cold knot tightening beneath my ribs. It was Mark. Mark, who used to dismantle my confidence brick by careful brick in the seventh grade. A friend request. My fingers hovered over the 'decline' button, trembling slightly. Every shadow in the room seemed to shift, every creak in the floorboards sounded like footsteps approaching. I checked the lock on my door for the fifth time, the metal cool against my palm. Why would he do this now? What was he after? I leaned closer to the monitor, my breath catching in my throat, convinced the glow of the screen was somehow broadcasting his presence into my space.

[story 2]

I stared at the incoming request as if it were a venomous insect crawling across the glass. My heart began to hammer against my sternum, a frantic rhythm that drowned out the distant traffic noise. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my small apartment, running a hand through my hair until it felt rough against my scalp. Each movement felt exaggerated, overly cautious. I kept glancing toward the window, half-expecting to see a familiar silhouette lurking down the street. The air in the room felt thick, heavy, suffocating. If I accepted, what would happen? I couldn't shake the feeling that this wasn't a simple digital invitation; it felt like a trap sprung, a silent signal confirming that the past hadn't just retreated—it was actively stalking me again.

[story 3]

The chime sounded, and I froze mid-sip of my tea. It was him. That familiar, irritatingly smug face staring back at me from the notification. A wave of intense unease washed over me, making my skin prickle. I immediately backed away from my desk, bumping against the bookshelf, the sudden noise making me jump. My gaze darted around the room, cataloging every object—the placement of the books, the angle of the lamp, the faint dust motes dancing in the single beam of light. It felt like the room itself was conspiring against me. I didn't move toward the message; instead, I grabbed my phone, my knuckles white, and started scrolling through every contact list, searching for an escape route, a way to sever this invisible tether to that awful time.
